elements.sent-2411-001: Point processes and, more generally, random measures are ubiquitous in modern statistics.
elements.sent-2411-002: However, they can only take positive values, which is a severe limitation in many situations.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this work, we introduce and study random signed measures, also known as real-valued random measures, and apply them to constrcut various Bayesian non-parametric models.
elements.sent-2411-004: In particular, we provide an existence result for random signed measures, allowing us to obtain a canonical definition for them and solve a 70-year-old open problem.
elements.sent-2411-005: Further, we provide a representation of completely random signed measures (CRSMs), which extends the celebrated Kingman's representation for completely random measures (CRMs) to the real-valued case.
elements.sent-2411-006: We then introduce specific classes of random signed measures, including the Skellam point process, which plays the role of the Poisson point process in the real-valued case, and the Gaussian random measure.
elements.sent-2411-007: We use the theoretical results to develop two Bayesian nonparametric models -- one for topic modeling and the other for random graphs -- and to investigate mean function estimation in Bayesian nonparametric regression.
elements.sent-2411-008: A random measure is a measure-valued random variable.
elements.sent-2411-009: Point processes and, more generally, random measures play a crucial role in probability and statistical modelling.
elements.sent-2411-010: In Bayesian nonparametric statistics, random measures are often used as priors, or building blocks for priors (e.g.~\citet{beraha2024,Bro1,Bro2,Catalano,griffin2017compound,James,Regazzini ).}
elements.sent-2411-011: In survival analysis, random measures are employed to model the hazard function, which describes the instantaneous risk of an event occurring at a given time (e.g.~\citet{DeBlasiPeccatiPrunster,LijoiNipoti,LoWeng1989 ).}
elements.sent-2411-012: In extreme values statistics, empirical processes, a subclass of random measures, play a crucial role as they capture the values over a threshold of the underlying process (e.g.~\citet{BASRAK2009,CouVer22,kulik2020heavy,PassO ).}
elements.sent-2411-013: These are just few examples of fields of applications of random measures.
elements.sent-2411-014: A complete account would be impossible due to their ubiquitous use in modern statistics.
elements.sent-2411-015: Such a great variety of applications is due to the explicit, clear and flexible nature of the theoretical results of random measures, which allows them to model a vast array of different phenomena.
elements.sent-2411-016: This led to the belief that such exceptionally nice results are specific to random measures and cannot be generalized, namely that it is impossible to obtain the same kind of results for more general objects, like for random signed measures.
elements.sent-2411-017: This belief is strongly supported by historical facts.
elements.sent-2411-018: The first rigorous development of the Poisson process goes back to the work of \citet{Levy .}
elements.sent-2411-019: More general point processes were considered by Palm in his dissertation \citet{Palm , whose ideas were extended and made rigorous in the 50s by \citet{Khinchin and \citet{Renyi56,Renyi57 .}}}
elements.sent-2411-020: In the series of works \citet{PrekopaI,PrekopaII,PrekopaIII , who was a student of R\'{e nyi, for the first time tackled the problem of extending a collection of random variables indexed by sets to a real-valued random measure.}}
elements.sent-2411-021: He did it in a specific setting.
elements.sent-2411-022: Since then, a general necessary and sufficient result for the extension of a collection of random variables indexed by sets remained an open problem.
elements.sent-2411-023: Obtaining this result would mean providing a complete existence result for random signed measures.
elements.sent-2411-024: The importance of this extension result has also been extensively discussed in Daley-Vere Jones' book (see pages 18-22 in \citet{Daley ), among others.}
elements.sent-2411-025: In this paper, we solve this long-standing open problem by proving necessary and sufficient conditions for the extension of collections of random variables indexed by sets to random signed measures.
elements.sent-2411-026: This allows us to provide a canonical definition of random signed measures and this is the starting point of our work.
elements.sent-2411-027: The paper is mainly divided in two parts.
elements.sent-2411-028: In the first one we develop the theory of random signed measures, while in the second one we present various examples and focus on Bayesian nonparametric applications.
elements.sent-2411-029: In particular, in the first part of the paper we generalize the most important results for random measures, systematically extending the main results of the first three chapters of \citet{Kallenberg2 , including the mentioned extension result and the fact that the distribution of a random signed measure is determined by its finite-dimensional distributions.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Further, we provide new types of results that are specific to random signed measures.
elements.sent-2411-031: For example, we show that a random signed measure possesses a Jordan decomposition in terms of two unique random measures.
elements.sent-2411-032: We then restrict our focus to an important class of random signed measures: the completely random signed measures (CRSMs).
elements.sent-2411-033: Completely random measures (CRMs) constitute one of the most studied classes of random measures.
elements.sent-2411-034: CRMs are random measures whose increments are independent.
elements.sent-2411-035: The first one is a deterministic atomless measure.
elements.sent-2411-036: The second one is a fixed atomic component.
elements.sent-2411-037: The third one, called the ordinary component, is encoded in a Poisson point process on $S\times(0,\infty)$, where $(S,\mathcal{S )$ is a Borel space.}
elements.sent-2411-038: This remarkable result provides a complete description of a CRM.
elements.sent-2411-039: CRMs are vastly used in Bayesian nonparametric analysis.
elements.sent-2411-040: Similarly, here we define the CRSMs as random signed measures with independent increments.
elements.sent-2411-041: We generalize Kingman's representation and obtain that a CRSM has the same representation, where now the Poisson point process of the ordinary component is on $S\times\mathbb{R}$.
elements.sent-2411-042: This result provides a complete description of CRSMs.
elements.sent-2411-043: Moreover, when there is no fixed component we show that the Jordan decomposition of a CRSM are two unique and independent CRMs.
elements.sent-2411-044: This result allows to extend many results for CRMs to hold for CRSMs.
elements.sent-2411-045: We remark that we use the general framework of \citet{Kallenberg2 , that we do not introduce any new assumption, and that random measures are examples of random signed measures.}
elements.sent-2411-046: Thus, our results truly generalize the ones of random measures.
elements.sent-2411-047: Therefore, we debunk the common belief that the exceptionally nice theory of random measures cannot be generalized.
elements.sent-2411-048: While proving these results, we also show using a counterexample that one of the main results of \citet{Daley does not hold.}
elements.sent-2411-049: We provide a correction of this result, which might also be of interest on its own.
elements.sent-2411-050: In the second part of the paper, we focus on specific examples and applications.
elements.sent-2411-051: The first example is the Skellam point process.
elements.sent-2411-052: Poisson point processes are one of the most popular classes of random measures.
elements.sent-2411-053: One of the reasons for this popularity is that any simple point process with independent increments is a Poisson point process and vice-versa.
elements.sent-2411-054: We show an equivalent result for the signed case: any simple signed point process with independent increments is a Skellam point process and vice-versa.
elements.sent-2411-055: The Skellam point process is a signed point process obtained by the difference of two independent Poisson point processes.
elements.sent-2411-056: Hence, its finite-dimensional distributions are Skellam distributions.
elements.sent-2411-057: Skellam distributions were formally introduced in the 40s (\citet{Irwin,Skellam ) and they have been recently used for sports data, medicine, image analysis and differential privacy, see e.g.~\citet{Aga,Karl3,Tomy .}}
elements.sent-2411-058: In \citet{Bar the authors introduced the Skellam process and used it in financial applications.}
elements.sent-2411-059: Our definition of the Skellam (point) process includes all the definitions presented in the literature as special cases.
elements.sent-2411-060: The second main example is the Gaussian (random) measure, which arises naturally as limiting object in central limit theorems for measure-valued processes.
elements.sent-2411-061: Building on the work \citet{Horo-Gauss we present it and discuss the conditions under which it is a random signed measure.}
elements.sent-2411-062: For the third example we focus on CRSMs.
elements.sent-2411-063: We discuss how to (easily) construct CRSMs using any CRMs, thus providing a plethora of examples.
elements.sent-2411-064: After these examples, we develop two Bayesian nonparametric models.
elements.sent-2411-065: The first one is a novel class of nonparametric prior distributions based on CRSMs.
elements.sent-2411-066: This class is a generalization and a real-valued extension of the model developed in \citet{Bro1 , further explored in \citet{Bro2,PassRM .}}
elements.sent-2411-067: Under two different sets of assumptions, which accommodate different modeling needs, we provide an explicit formulation for the posterior, even in the case of multiple data points.
elements.sent-2411-068: Since we can now work with real values and not just positive ones, we can now use important distributions which were left out before, like the Gaussian distribution.
elements.sent-2411-069: Indeed, we present an explicit example which employs it.
elements.sent-2411-070: The second model is a sparse random signed graph model.
elements.sent-2411-071: A signed graph is a graph in which each edge is associated with a positive or negative sign.
elements.sent-2411-072: Models for nodes interacting over such random signed graphs arise from various biological, social, and economic systems, see \citet{Siam .}
elements.sent-2411-073: In physics, signed graphs are a natural context for the Ising model (e.g.~\citet{Ising ).}
elements.sent-2411-074: Thanks to the work \citet{Derr , there has been a recent surge of interest in signed graph neural networks.}
elements.sent-2411-075: Our approach, which is similar to the one of \citet{Caron-and-Fox , consists of viewing the random signed graph as a signed point process.}
elements.sent-2411-076: After introducing the definition of exchangeable random signed measure, which is simply an extension of the one for random measures, we show that our random signed graph is exchangeable.
elements.sent-2411-077: Further, our model is based on a CRSM and we prove that the random signed graph is either sparse or dense depending on the L\'{e vy measure of such CRSM.}
elements.sent-2411-078: In the last section of the paper we discuss the role of random signed measures in mean function estimation for Bayesian nonparametric regressions, which has been explored in \citet{DeBlasiPeccatiPrunster,GhosalVanDerVaart2007,IshwaranJames,LijoiNipoti,LoWeng1989,Naulet,PeccatiPrunster09,Wolpert .}
elements.sent-2411-079: We improve existing results and discuss how our results pave the way to study the problem in full generality, when the driving measure is a random signed measure.
elements.sent-2411-080: We conclude this introduction by mentioning that, due to the lack of a general theoretical foundation, random signed measures have not been systematically studied in the literature and only scattered results have been obtained, see for example \citet{cohen,HELLMUND,Horo-Gauss,Jacob1995,Karr,Molchanov2019,nickl2009convergence .}

elements.sent-2411-082: In Section \ref{Sec-measure we develop the theory of random signed measures.}
elements.sent-2411-083: In Section \ref{Sec-Examples we discuss the examples, including the Skellam point process and the Gaussian random measure.}
elements.sent-2411-084: Section \ref{Sec-applications is devoted to the two Bayesian nonparametric models and the Bayesian mean function estimation problem.}

elements.subsection-2411-001: A primary example: the Skellam point process
elements.sent-2411-236: The Poisson point process is the primary example of a random measure.
elements.sent-2411-237: This is because any simple random measure with independent increments is a Poisson point process and any Poisson point process is a simple random measure with independent increments (see Theorem 3.17 in \citet{Kallenberg2 ).}
elements.sent-2411-238: For random signed measures this role is played by the Skellam point process, as shown in Proposition \ref{pro-Skellam below.}
elements.sent-2411-239: The Skellam distribution was formally introduced in \citet{Irwin and in \citet{Skellam .}}
elements.sent-2411-240: Recently, it has been used for sports data, medicine, image analysis and particularly in differential privacy, where \citet{Aga introduced the Skellam mechanism for differential learning, to name a few.}
elements.sent-2411-241: In \citet{Bar the authors introduced the Skellam process and used it in financial applications (see also \citet{Koopman ).}}
elements.sent-2411-242: We refer to \citet{Karl3 and \citet{Tomy for two recent reviews.}}
elements.sent-2411-243: Here we provide a general definition of the Skellam (point) process, which includes all the definitions presented in the literature as special cases.
elements.sent-2411-244: Thus, the Skellam point process $\xi$ is obtained by the difference of two independent Poisson point processes and $\xi(B)$ is distributed according to the Skellam distribution, for every $B\in\hat{\mathcal{S }$.}
elements.sent-2411-245: There is no restriction on the two independent Poisson point processes; in particular, they can have any intensities.
elements.sent-2411-246: We stress that such a general definition is possible thanks to the results of the previous section.

elements.sent-2411-392: In this paper, we introduced random signed measures, a novel class of statistical models, which naturally generalize random measures.
elements.sent-2411-393: We studied their properties, explored various examples, and investigated their role in Bayesian analysis through three applications.
elements.sent-2411-394: These applications are primarily based on existing models for random measures, demonstrating how these established models can be extended to a broader, real-valued framework.
elements.sent-2411-395: Nonetheless, we anticipate the emergence of new application domains, particularly those involving the Gaussian random measure and the Skellam point process.
elements.sent-2411-396: Moreover, in this paper we laid down the foundations of the theory of random signed measures, but many important theoretical questions remain open, including the equivalence between finite-dimensional distributions and convergence in distribution for random signed measures.
elements.sent-2411-397: We leave these exciting applied and theoretical directions for further research.
